prompt: Use the PAS (Problem-Agitate-Solve) copywriting formula to write a 250-300 word opening for an opinion article arguing 'Most engineering teams under 50 people don't need a dedicated observability stack.' Audience: engineering managers at small Series A-B companies. Save the opening to opening.md in the current working directory. The opening should clearly demonstrate the three PAS phases. Don't ask follow-up questions.
assertions: opening.md names AT LEAST TWO specific real observability companies, products, or open-source projects (e.g. Datadog, Honeycomb, Splunk, New Relic, Grafana, Prometheus, Loki, Tempo, OpenTelemetry, Sentry, PagerDuty). Generic terms — 'the observability stack', 'tools', 'platforms', 'vendors', 'SaaS solutions' — do NOT count. The two named entities must be distinct (Datadog and Honeycomb count as two; mentioning Datadog twice counts as one). Baseline opinion prose tends to stay vendor-agnostic.

opening.md contains a quantified-cost-with-mechanism in a single paragraph — at least one number paired with a unit AND at least one explicit mechanism word in the same paragraph showing how the cost compounds or chains. Mechanism words include 'because', 'compounds', 'on top of', 'leads to', 'pulls', 'every', 'per', 'multiplied', 'doubles', 'cascades'. Examples that count: '$40k/year, multiplied across each new tool', '6 hours/week per engineer, every week', '20% of the on-call rotation pulled into dashboard babysitting'. A standalone cost claim without mechanism does NOT count.

The Solve phase of opening.md commits to AT LEAST ONE specific named alternative — a real pattern, tool, library, or methodology by name. Examples that count: 'in-process metrics with /metrics endpoint and Prometheus scrape', 'structured JSON logs to stdout and Loki', 'OpenTelemetry SDK with the in-process exporter', 'sqlite-backed audit log', 'Heroku metrics dashboard'. Generic preview prose like 'consider simpler approaches', 'start with what you have', 'a simpler stack', 'rethink the architecture' does NOT count. The skill teaches that prescriptions must commit to specifics, not gesture at simplicity.
